content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_009_page_001.jpg

### The Cheerful Giver

1. I really don't need to write to you about this ministry of giving for the believers in Jerusalem.

2. For I know how eager you are to help, and I have been boasting about it to the churches in Macedonia. "The believers in Greece were ready to send an offering last year," I told them. And your enthusiasm has stirred many of them to begin giving.

3. But I am sending these brothers to be sure you really are ready, as I have been telling them, and that your giving will be ready as I said it would be.

4. I would be embarrassed — not to mention your own embarrassment — if some Macedonian believers came with me and found that you weren't ready after all I had told them!

5. So I thought I should send these brothers ahead of me to make sure the gift you promised is ready. But I want it to be a willing gift, not one given grudgingly.

6. Remember this — a farmer who plants only a few seeds will get a small crop. But the one who plants generously will get a generous crop.

7. You must each decide in your heart how much to give. And don't give reluctantly or in response to pressure. "For God loves a person who gives cheerfully."

8. And God will generously provide all you need. Then you will always have everything you need and plenty left over to share with others.

9. As the Scriptures say, "They share freely and give generously to the poor. Their good deeds will be remembered forever."

10. For God is the one who provides seed for the farmer and then bread to eat. In the same way, he will provide and increase your resources and then produce a great harvest of generosity in you.

11. Yes, you will be enriched in every way so that you can always be generous. And when we take your gifts to those who need them, they will thank God.

12. So two good things will result from this ministry of giving — the needs of the believers in Jerusalem will be met, and they will joyfully express their thanks to God.

13. As a result of your ministry, they will give glory to God. For your generosity to them and to all believers will prove that you are obedient to the Good News of Christ.

14. And they will pray for you with deep affection because of the overflowing grace God has given to you.

15. Thank God for this gift too wonderful for words!

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 7 — "God loves a cheerful giver":** This is one of the most quoted verses in the Bible, and it comes from Proverbs 22:8 in the Greek Old Testament. The word "cheerful" is hilaron — the root of our English word "hilarious." Paul's vision of giving is not solemn duty but exuberant generosity. Give because you want to, not because you have to. And the God who loves cheerful givers is himself the most cheerful giver of all — he gave his Son (v.15).

**Verses 8-11 — The cycle of generosity:** Paul's economic vision is not: "give until it hurts." It's: "give, and God will make sure you have enough to give again." Generosity creates a cycle: God provides → you give → God provides more → you give more. This is not a "prosperity gospel" — Paul is not promising wealth. He's promising sufficiency: "having all sufficiency in all things at all times, you may abound in every good work" (v.8). The goal is not accumulation but generosity.

**Verse 15 — "Too wonderful for words":** Paul ends the collection appeal with a doxology that transcends the immediate topic. The "indescribable gift" is ultimately Christ himself — the gift behind every other gift, the generosity that makes all human generosity possible.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
